coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive and selective electrochemical aptasensor based on rGO-MWCNTs/Chitosan/carbon quantum dot for the detection of lysozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Behzad
Jamei, Hamid Reza
Ensafi, Ali Asghar
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptamer
Lysozyme
Electrochemical aptasensor
Reduced graphene oxide
Multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Chitosan
Carbon quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GO and rGO nanomaterials
Synthesis of amino-functionalized CQDs from chitosan
Characterization of rGO and GO nanosheets
Characterization of amino-functionalized CQDs from chitosan
Evaluation of the electrochemical properties of all applied nanocomposites
Surface morphology of nanocomposites on GCE
Optimization of parameters in preparation of aptasensor
Reproducibility and storage stability of the anti-lysozyme aptasensor
Application of aptasensor for lysozyme detection in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: the Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Office of Vice Chancellor for Research and Technology, University of Isfahan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT), the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council for their support and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic of the step-wise preparation of GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT. (B) DPV, (C) CV, (D) EIS curves of (a) bare GCE, and modified GCE with (b) GO, (c) rGO, (d) rGO- MWCNT, (e) rGO-MWCNT/CS, (f) rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposites in a solution containing 2.5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (1:1) mixture and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl in Tris buffer.
FE-SEM (A–D) and TEM images (F-H) of Unmodified GCE (A), MWCNTs (B and F), synthesized rGO (C and E), rGO-MWCNT nanocomposite (D and G), and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposite (H).

(A) DPV and (B) EIS curves of (a) bare GCE, (b) nano-modified GCE of rGO, (c) immobilized aptamer on the nano-modified GCE of rGO, and (d) aptamer fitted to lysozyme (0.002 nmol L−1). (C) DPV and (D) EIS curves of (a) bare GCE, (b) nano-modified GCE of rGO-MWCNT, (c) immobilized aptamer on the nano-modified GCE of rGO-MWCNT, and (d) aptamer fitted to lysozyme (0.002 nmol L−1). (E) DPV and (F) EIS curves of (a) bare GCE, (b) nano-modified GCE of rGO-MWCNT/CS, (c) immobilized aptamer on the nano-modified GCE of rGO-MWCNT/CS, and (d) aptamer fitted to lysozyme (0.002 nmol L−1). (G) DPV and (H) EIS curves of (a) bare GCE, (b) nano-modified GCE of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD, (c) immobilized aptamer on the nano-modified GCE of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD, and (d) aptamer fitted to lysozyme (0.002 nmol L−1). (I) I
p and (J) R
ct variation for each step in detection of lysozyme was measured via prepared aptasensor, in which rGO, rGO-MWCNT, rGO-MWCNT/CS, and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD were applied as sensitive layers. DPV and EIS curves acquired in a solution containing 2.5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (1:1) mixture and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl in TE buffer (pH 7.4).

(A) DPV voltammograms of anti-lysozyme aptasensor-based rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD for different concentrations of lysozyme within 20 fmol L−1–10 nmol L−1, (B) calibration curve for ΔI and (C) ΔI vs. log(Clysozyme, nmol L−1). (D) Nyquist plots of anti-lysozyme aptasensor-based rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD for different concentrations of lysozyme within 10 fmol L−1–100 nmol L−1, (E) calibration curve for ΔR
CT and (F) ΔR
CT vs. log(Clysozyme, nmol L−1).
Comparison of developed electrochemical aptasensors for detection of lysozyme.
Recovery measurements of lysozyme for real sample analysis in the presence of developed aptasensor.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive and selective electrochemical aptasensor based on rGO-MWCNTs/Chitosan/carbon quantum dot for the detection of lysozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111 I.R., Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the increase of diseases especially cancer is a major global concern in such a way that more than 14 million cancer cases have been estimated around the word during the last decade. Moreover, it is expected that this number will increase to 24 million by 2035 (Stewart and Wild, 2014). Accordingly, the identification and quantification of bio-samples' proteins, biomarkers, and tumor markers is very important for early diagnosis of diseases and cancers for more effective treatment. In addition, those play important roles in basic studies carried out on food safety (Besten et al., 2018), proteomics (Gianazza et al., 2014), bioengineering (Zhang et al., 2016), medical, and clinical applications (Yoo et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2017a). One of the bio-samples' proteins is lysozyme, which is a ubiquitous enzyme and plays an essential role in the various organisms and can be a tumor marker.

Lysozyme is highly effective protein in the immune system of the human body due to its antibacterial activity (Liu et al., 2011). Moreover, this protein is used in medicine and clinical fields as a biomarker, model in the pharmaceutical, replacement of antibiotics in veterinary medicine, the model in protein studies, and model for enzyme catalysis (Vasilescu et al., 2016). It is available in nature as a single polypeptide chain (composed of 129 amino acid residues), has a molecular weight of 14,300 Da, and an isoelectric point of 11.0. These attributes of lysozyme have made it a great analytical model for innovative techniques in proteomics (Vasilescu et al., 2016). Given the importance of the clinical detection of lysozyme, researchers have proposed different techniques for detection of lysozyme using aptasensor (aptamer-based sensors). These techniques include the colorimetric (Lou et al., 2017), electrochemiluminescent (Zhao et al., 2014b), optical sensor (Liu et al., 2016), surface plasmon resonance (Saylan et al., 2017), fluorescent (Chen et al., 2016), field effect transistors (Villamizar et al., 2008), quartz crystal microbalance (Sener et al., 2010) and electrochemical methods (Ocana et al., 2015; Heydari-Bafrooei et al., 2016a; Xiao et al., 2013a, 2013b; Cao et al., 2018). In this regard, the aptasensor is proved to be a powerful technique for the identification of biomolecules such as lysozyme (Vasilescu et al., 2016). Moreover, considerable efforts have been made to develop aptasensors using various identification methods. Among the various reported aptamer-based sensors, Electrochemical aptasensors, have the most intense public attention from scholars due to many advantages such as faster, more robust response, sensitivity, stability, more potential for miniaturization, portability, and cost-efficient nature compared with other methods (Iliuk et al., 2011).

At the present-day studies, for better immobilization of aptamer on the transducer surface, signal amplification, and increase the sensitivity, different conducting polymers and composites of different nanomaterials have been used for developing the electrochemical aptasensors (Sun et al., 2014; Ensafi et al., 2014a; Heydari-Bafrooei and Shamszadeha, 2017b). Numerous studies have revealed that combining aptamer with nanomaterials is successful for building and developing novel electrochemical aptasensor. In this regard, graphene-based nanocomposites have attracted increased attention due to the high electron mobility, synergistic contribution of functional components, high specific surface, high mechanical strength, low manufacturing cost, and other advantages (Xie et al., 2014; Qin et al., 2016; Fang et al., 2016). Moreover, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), as another class of nanoparticles, can be used as a modifier in the electrochemical aptasensor (Li et al., 2016; Heydari-Bafrooei et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2013a, 2013b), due to their good conductivity, specific surface areas, and high surface-to-volume ratios (Ensafi et al., 2016b), for better immobilization of aptamers.

In addition, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have highly desirable properties from the prospect of nanotechnology in the development of sensitive, stable, and cost-effective aptasensor (Gao et al., 2004). CQDs as small carbon nanoparticles (less than 10 nm in size) have become one of the most important nanomaterials in recent studies (Lim et al., 2015). CQDs can be considered as a potential material to enhance the immobilization of biological types (aptamer chains or DNA) because of their noticeable characteristics, including hydrophobicity, good chemical stability, susceptibility to chemical modifications, good relatively conductivity, biocompatibility, cost efficiency, and easy synthesis properties (Qu et al., 2013).

Different polymers have been used in the development and improvement of biosensors. Chitosan (CS) is a polycationic polymer with special properties including high adhesion, high film-forming ability and biocompatibility, and therefore can be used as a suitable matrix for immobilization of biomolecules (Tabasi et al., 2017). The non-conductive property of CS is a limitation for the development of electrochemical biosensors. However, the combination of CS with high conductive nanomaterials makes it suitable for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors.

At the present study, by considering the unique benefits of graphene, MWCNTs, CQDs nanomaterials and CS polymer, a combination of these materials is proposed for developing and enhancing the electrochemical aptasensor for detection of lysozyme protein. This combination was used in an aptasensor in order to apply synergy contributions and better immobilization of aptamer. In this regard, as a case study, we applied a 42 bases anti-lysozyme aptamer and electrochemical techniques such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to monitor lysozyme levels on the developed electrochemical aptasensors. In addition, in order to investigate the application of proposed electrochemical aptasensor, recovery test was used for detection of lysozyme in different real samples such as Egg white, Serum, Urine, and Wine.

The sequence of anti-lysozyme aptamer (APT) applied in this study is "5′-NH2-ATC TAC GAA TTC ATC AGG GCT AAA GAG TGC AGA GTT ACT TAG-3" (Xiao et al., 2013a, 2013b). The description of all reagents and chemical materials and apparatuses were presented in more detail in the "Supplementary Materials" file (Section S-1.1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GO and rGO nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO (rGO) from natural graphite powder, the modified Hummer's method was used (Xu et al., 2008). More details of preparation relating to GO and rGO nanosheets are presented in the "Supplementary Materials" file (Section S-1.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized CQDs from chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the CS was used to synthesize the amino-functionalized CQDs by hydrothermal carbonization (Yang et al., 2009; Rezaei et al., 2016). The synthesis of CQDs was explained in the "Supplementary Materials" file in more details (Section S-1.3).

In this process, 0.5 g of MWCNTs (with a length of 5–9 µm bundles and a diameter of 70 – 110 nm were purchased from Aldrich Chemical Company) was purified and activated by reflux in nitric acid. First, they were sonicated with 100 mL of HNO3 (5.0 mol L−1) under reflux condition for 16 h. After that, deionized water was used to dilute the acquired mixture of MWCNTs and that mixture was filtered. The resulting precipitate was washed with pure water until the outlet water from filter reached neutral pH. Then, the final product of MWCNTs, which has been functionalized to contain –COOH groups, was dried at room temperature.

For the preparation of rGO-MWCNTs/CS, 5.0 mg of MWCNTs and 5.0 mg of rGO (synthesized by modified Hummer's method) were dispersed in 5.0 mL of the 2% acetic acid (v/v) solution containing 0.5 mol L−1 of NaCl and 10.0 mg CS by ultrasonication of the solution for 1 h. Afterward, 5.0 mg of synthesized CQD (obtained from CS) was added to the dispersed rGO-MWCNT/CS solution and the resulting suspension was sonicated for 2 h. Eventually, the homogeneous suspension of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs nanocomposite was prepared (as shown in the schematic image, Fig. 1A).

In this process, for preparation of the biorecognition surface, glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) were first polished with 0.06 mm of alumina slurries. Afterward, they were ultrasonically washed in 50% (v/v) ethanol and water solution and then in distilled water. Then, 5.0 µL of the homogenous suspension rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs was dropped onto a GCE surface and dried at room temperature. For the covalent immobilization of the amino-linked lysozyme aptamer, the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs were incubated in a 2% glutaraldehyde (GLA) solution for 45 min and washed with Tris buffer. The GLA was covalently joined to the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs surface via its reaction with –NH2 groups of CS and CQDs.

Afterward, the GCE/rGO-MWCNTs/CS/CQDs were immersed in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4) containing 5.0 µM anti-lysozyme aptamer for 1.5 h. Therefore, anti-lysozyme aptamer was attached to the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs/GLA film through the formation of covalent bonds between the amino groups of anti-lysozyme aptamer and aldehyde groups (-COH) of GLA (as shown in the schematic image, Fig. 1A).

The prepared electrochemical aptasensors were washed with Tris buffer solution for 10 s to eliminate unbound aptamers from the modified GCE. Then, 100 µL of 0.2% BSA solution was dropped on the prepared electrochemical aptasensors for 1 h to deactivate the unreacted sites of the modified GCE and avoid nonspecific adsorption. Afterward, prepared GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs/GLA/APT was immersed in Tris buffer containing lysozyme with various concentrations for 1 h (pH 7.4). In order to detect changes in the electrochemical properties of the modified GCE, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques were applied as the electrochemical methods (as shown in the schematic image, Fig. 1A).

The performances of the modified electrode were investigated by aforementioned electrochemical methods in a three-electrode cell assembly, containing 2.5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (1:1) mixture as a redox probe and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl in Tris buffer (pH 7.4). The EIS measurements were carried out in a frequency range from 5 mHz to 100 kHz with a sinusoidal signal of 10 mV and at a DC potential of +0.15 V versus Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl). The CV technique was applied by a potential scanning between −0.2 and 0.6 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The DVP technique was used with operating conditions; including modulation amplitude of 50.0 mV, step potential of 8.0 mV, modulation time of 0.05 s, and interval time of 0.5 s. Using Savitzky and Golay filter (level 2) of the GPES software, the raw voltammograms of DPV method were treated. The average baseline correction was defined by a ‘peak width’ of 0.01 V. The measurements of these three electrochemical techniques were conducted using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT 12 device at room temperature as well as the GPES and FRA software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of rGO and GO nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM, TEM, FT-IR, XRD, EDS, and Raman spectroscopy techniques were used to investigate the characteristics of the exfoliated GO and rGO synthesized from natural graphite. The obtained results of this section were completely presented in the "Supplementary Materials" file (Section S-2.1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of amino-functionalized CQDs from chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: The DLS, XRD, TEM, FT-IR, UV–vis–NIR, and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques were applied to study the characteristics of amino-functionalized CQDs synthesized from chitosan. The obtained results of this section were explained in the "Supplementary Materials" file (Section S-2.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the electrochemical properties of all applied nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the electrochemical properties of GO, rGO, rGO-MWCNT, rGO-MWCNT/CS, and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposites were evaluated. In this regard, for each aforementioned nanocomposite, the CV, EIS, and DPV techniques were applied for each modification step on GCE. Fig. 1B shows the DPVs of 2.5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (1:1) and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl in 10 mM Tris buffer on the different GCE covered with GO, rGO, rGO-MWCNT, rGO-MWCNT/CS, and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposites. The unmodified GCE has a discrete peak (trace a). The GO nanosheet coated on GCE was led to a sharp decrease in the peak current (trace b). Due to the existence of many functional groups in GO, which hamper the transfer of electrons between the electrode surface and electrolyte, the electrical conductivity of GO is low. After covering the GCE surface with rGO nanosheet, the current response clearly increases (trace c). However, rGO-MWCNT can enhance the electron transfer characteristics and increases the GCE surface (trace d), which leads to an increase in the current response compared with GO. This result is because of the high conductivity, and the large surface area in rGO-MWCNT, and remaining oxygen-related defects properties in rGO. The rGO-MWCNT/CS nanocomposite coated on GCE wasn’t led to intensive changes in the peak current (trace e) compared with rGO-MWCNT. By modifying the electrode with rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD, the current response increases more than other previous states (trace f). This nanocomposite increases the electron-transfer ability and GCE surface unique benefits of graphene, MWCNTs, CQDs nanomaterials CS polymer, and its synergy contributions.

The outcome of this section shows that the rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD electrochemical properties are greater than GO, rGO, rGO-MWCNT, and rGO-MWCNT/CS nanocomposites. It should be noted that the results of electrochemical analysis relating to only GCE-CS and GCE-CQD were not discussed in this study because the obtained results did not show a sensible change in electrochemical measurements in comparison with unmodified GCE. Additionally, CV outputs (Fig. 1C) confirm differential pulse voltammetry outputs acquired under similar conditions. After that, EIS measurements were acquired for different nano-modified GCEs under similar conditions. This study showed that due to the existence of MWCNTs, CS, and rGO in nanocomposites, the amount of R
ct has been sharply reduced, in comparison with bare and GO modified electrode. This can be attributed to the great conducting capability of MWCNTs and rGO. Moreover, as presented in the Fig. 1D, after coating rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposite on the surface GCE, the sensible change is not observed in R
ct in comparison with GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS. The resulting data from EIS was fitted in an equivalent circuit using Z-View software as shown in Fig. S-3 and its details are described in “Supplementary Materials” file (Section S-2.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of nanocomposites on GCE
ce:section.ce:para: FE-SEM and TEM are the powerful techniques for investigating the morphology of a surface on a nano-scale. Fig. 2 shows the surface morphologies of each modified working GCE in FE-SEM and TEM. Fig. 2A displays the surface roughness of unmodified GCE. The SEM and TEM images of MWCNTs film show a good dispersion in Fig. 2B and F, respectively. The SEM (Fig. 2C) and TEM (Fig. 2E) images show that rGO nanosheets were thin. As seen, the impressive surface area of the modified GCE has increased by deploying well conductive MWCNTs compared with unmodified GCE. In addition, the existence of MWCNTs in the nanocomposites may facilitate mass-transfer and charge-transfer processes. Fig. 2D and G show the morphology images of rGO-MWCNTs relating to SEM and TEM, respectively. According to these figures, MWCNTs were well put into rGO nanosheets and showed good dispersion and a homogeneous configuration. Fig. 2H (TEM) display that the modified GCE surface of rGO-MWCNTs/CS/CQD nanocomposite is visible and CQDs appear to enhance the surface area of rGO-MWCNTs. Furthermore, CQDs could be detected on the sidewalls of rGO-MWCNTs/CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters in preparation of aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the effective experimental parameters in preparation of electrochemical aptasensors were optimized to acquire the best analytical performance of electrochemical aptasensors. The obtained optimum values of rGO to MWCNTs ratio (w/w%), aptamer concentration, aptamer immobilization time, aptasensor retention time in lysozyme solution, and rGO-MWCNT to CS ratio (w/w%) parameters were 1.0 mg mL−1 (1:1), 5.0 µmol L−1,100 min, 60 min, and 1:2, respectively. The results of this section are explained in “Supplementary Materials” file in more details (Section S-2.4).

EIS and DPV methods for samples at different steps and lysozyme detection technique were carried out using 2.5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (1:1) mixture as a redox probe and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl in Tris buffer. The aptamer immobilization process on the modified GCE surface of nanomaterials restricts the access of electrons to the transistor surface, which leads to the low efficiency of electron transfer. Subsequently, this process leads to the increase of R
ct in the EIS and decrease of peak current (I
p ) in the DPV measurements. Moreover, after lysozyme detection with anti-lysozyme aptasensor, I
p decreases continuously and R
ct increases because lysozyme additionally restricts the access of electrons to the surface of modified GCE.

The rGO, rGO-MWCNT, rGO-MWCNT/CS, and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD were used for the fabrication of the anti-lysozyme aptasensor. The immobilization amount of APT and the performance of lysozyme detection on the modified GCE with these nanocomposites were compared with each other. Fig. 3A-H show the DPV and EIS behaviors of the aforementioned nanocomposites in preparation of aptasensor. In addition, Fig. 3I-J show the variation of lysozyme detection performance on the different modified GCE surfaces by R
ct and I
p values. The differences between R
ct and I
p values (ΔR and ΔI) were considered as the main responses for after and before preparation of a new adhesive layer.

For aptasensors based on rGO, rGO-MWCNT, rGO-MWCNT/CS, and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposites, the outcomes demonstrate that the ΔI values after lysozyme detection (Clysozyme = 0.002 nmol L−1) are 1.21, 1.47, 5.28, and 10.31 µA, respectively (Fig. 3I). Besides, the values of the ΔR are 4.71, 12.16, 182.24, and 293.11 Ω (Fig. 3J), respectively, for the applied nanocomposites. The considerable increase in the values of ΔR and ΔI at rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD, compared to other nanomodified GCE, is appropriate for the lysozyme identification. Fig. 3I-J show that, after the immobilization of aptamer on the rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD and catching the lysozyme, the most increase and decrease can be seen in R
ct and I
p , respectively. These obtained results show the better immobilization of aptamer due to the existence of the amino functional group in synthesized CQDs and CS. This result suggests a novel prospect for applying such films in the detection of protein by aptasensors.

To obtain the calibration curves, various concentrations of lysozyme were prepared in Tris buffer and identified by GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT. Fig. 4A shows the DPV voltammograms for different lysozyme concentrations. As shown in this figure, the peak currents decreased as the lysozyme concentration increased. Moreover, Fig. 4B presents the calibration curve for various concentrations of lysozyme. The peak current is inversely proportional to the lysozyme concentrations in a wide range of measurement from 20 fmol L−1 to 10 nmol L−1. The detection limit for lysozyme concentration is 3.7 fmol L−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The peak currents were equivalent to the logarithm of lysozyme concentration in the wide range of measurement from 20 fmol L−1 to 10 nmol L−1, as shown in Fig. 4C.

Fig. 4D displays the EIS spectra of GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT, after binding with different concentrations of lysozyme in Tris buffer. By binding the lysozyme protein with aptamer, electron transfer on the modified electrode surface and its conductivity decrease. Fig. 4E presents the calibration curve for various concentrations of lysozyme. Therefore, the R
ct linearly increases when the lysozyme concentration increases in a logarithmic curve (Fig. 4F). The resulting calibration curve from EIS technique for detection of lysozyme shows that the range of linear behavior is larger (10 fmol L−1 to 100 nmol L−1) than that of the calibration curve of DPV technique. According to EIS calibration curve, the range of lysozyme concentration is from 10 fmol L−1 to 100 nmol L−1 and the detection limit is 1.9 fmol L−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Table 1 presents the comparison of results of this study with other researches which have published up to now in the detection of lysozyme. The GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT obtained higher sensitivity, good selectivity, a lower detection limit, and strong specificity using applied EIS and DPV techniques. Therefore, this developed aptasensor can be as a successful aptasensor for detection of protein in clinical applications.

In this section, some proteins in the same family as lysozyme, like BSA, human IgG, BHb (Bovine Hemoglobin), and thrombin, were used to evaluate the selectivity of the developed aptasensor. In addition, they were applied to determine any probable non-specific adsorption of other proteins onto the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT. The mixture of these proteins with lysozyme has been tested under the same experimental conditions. As observed in Fig. S-5 (A-B), the changes of peak current in the DPV and R
ct in the EIS of the anti-lysozyme aptasensor related to the addition of BSA (0.02 nmol L−1), human IgG (0.01 nmol L−1), BHb (0.02 nmol L−1) and thrombin (0.003 nmol L−1) does not show considerable changes in comparison with the anti-lysozyme aptasensor related to the lysozyme (0.2 pmol L−1). This suggests that the proposed anti-lysozyme aptasensor has good selectivity in detecting between lysozyme and other different proteins. In addition, as shown in Fig. S-6, the electrochemical changes (ΔR and ΔI) of the aptasensor for lysozyme (0.2 pmol L−1) was much stronger than other applied proteins (20.0 pmol L−1) which were insignificant. The results indicated that the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT can just identify the lysozyme protein and couldn’t combine with the other proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and storage stability of the anti-lysozyme aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the developed aptasensor is important in the assay of protein. Ten parallel-prepared aptasensors (GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT) were applied for identifying 0.002 nmol L−1 lysozyme (Section S-2.6).

The signals of ten prepared GCE/rGO-MWCNTs/CS/CQD/APT for detection of 0.002 nmol L−1 lysozyme are shown in Table S1. A Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) of 4.2% was evaluated for the value of ΔI. Moreover, RSD was computed for ΔR and was equal to 3.8% (Table S-1). In order to estimate the repeatability of the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT, three detections were replicated for one aptasensor. The RSD of this test was 4.7% that presented the good repeatability for this aptasensor.

In the practical application of the detection of the lysozyme protein, the storage stability of the prepared GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT is an important issue. To assess the stability of the anti-lysozyme aptasensor, Fig. S-7 is provided to present the time-dependency of the relative current response change, related to the original signal of 2.0 pmol L−1 lysozyme on the first day of measurement. The aptasensors were kept in dry conditions at a temperature of 4 °C between each measurement on different days. The results showed that, in lysozyme presence, after one month, the anti-lysozyme aptasensor signal for 2.0 pmol L−1 lysozyme is 91% of the original signal (Fig. S-7). This outcome reveals that the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT has good stability (Section S-2.7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of aptasensor for lysozyme detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, the lysozyme content in egg white, serum, and urine of healthy people and wine was tested to examine the feasibility of applying GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT in the real sample analysis by DPV after preparation ( Table 2). For this purpose, 1.0 g of egg white was diluted 1000 times in Tris buffer; serum, urine, and wine were provided from healthy persons and diluted 20, 5, and 2 times in Tris buffer, respectively. Then, the prepared aptasensor was immersed into the each of the real samples for 1.5 h. The lysozyme concentrations were 2.23 ± 0.07 nM, 4.61 ± 0.09 nM, 0.52 ± 0.05 nM (n = 4) for egg white, serum and urine, respectively (wine: C
lysozyme
˂LOD). These measurements were located into the normal range of the reported values [8]. By applying analytical recovery experiments, the accuracy of the assay was tested, as shown in Table 2. According to the spike standards method, the percentage of spiked recoveries changed from 94.0 to 104.3. The results confirmed the efficiency of this technique for lysozyme analysis in real and biological samples.

This study has developed a novel electrochemical aptasensor based on GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposite for detection of lysozyme protein. In the developed aptasensor, in lysozyme presence, the aptamer on adsorbent layer catches the target lysozyme on the electrode interface. This process makes a barrier for electrons and restricts electron transfer; therefore, it resulted in a decrease of peak currents in DPV and an increase of R
ct in EIS for the GCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT. The results demonstrated that the immobilization amount of aptamer molecules on the modified GCE surface with rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposite has increased. Moreover, the GCE-/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT offers a low LOD compared to other aptasensors for the detection of lysozyme. Although the fabrication of nanoparticles and CQDs is not straightforward and fabrication of anti-lysozyme GCE-/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/APT has complex stages, they have several advantages for biomolecule (aptamer) immobilization, including (i) the existence of hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) functional groups in synthetic CQD, rGO-MWCNT, and chitosan; (ii) the existence of many amino (-NH2) functional groups in synthetic CQD and chitosan, and positively charged chitosan; (iii) fast electron-transfer kinetics and high conductivity which is due to the presence of MWCNTs and rGO (remaining oxygen-related defects properties in rGO); (iv) high surface-to-volume ratio in the presence of CQDs and rGO-MWCNT. Additionally, the anti-lysozyme aptasensor presents several advantages compared to other proposed electrochemical aptasensors in recent years, such as great stability, selectivity, and sensitivity. The adopted nanocomposite approach in the electrochemical aptasensor of this study could be applied for the detection of small molecules and other different proteins. This approach, based on aptamer, can be a powerful and selective tool for improving and developing novel electrochemical aptasensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT), the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council for their support and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.05.012.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Ocana, C., Hayat, A., Mishra, R., Vasilescu, A., del Valle, M., Marty, J.L., 2015. (Cambridge, U K), 140, pp. 4148–4153.

sb:publisher.sb:name: International Agency for Research on Cancer
